## How to Calculate

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: Gravitational potential energy is the energy an object possesses due to its position relative to a reference point (usually the ground). It's calculated using mass, gravity, and height.

faq.q: How does height affect gravitational potential energy?
faq.a: Potential energy increases proportionally with height. Double the height, double the potential energy. This is why objects at higher elevations have more stored energy.

faq.q: What's the difference between potential and kinetic energy?
faq.a: Potential energy depends on position (height), while kinetic energy depends on motion (velocity). When an object falls, potential energy converts to kinetic energy.

faq.q: Does the path taken affect potential energy?
faq.a: No. Potential energy only depends on the starting and ending heights, not the path taken. This makes it a conservative force.

faq.q: Can I calculate potential energy on other planets?
faq.a: Yes. Change the gravity value (g). On the Moon: g = 1.62 m/s². On Jupiter: g = 24.79 m/s². The formula stays the same.
Yes. All our calculators are completely free to use with no registration required.
Very accurate for standard conditions using validated physics formulas. However, for critical applications, consult a professional.

faq.q: What happens to potential energy when an object falls?
faq.a: As an object falls, its potential energy decreases while kinetic energy increases. The total (Ep + Ek) remains constant in a frictionless system.

faq.q: Why is gravity 9.81 m/s² and not 10?
faq.a: 9.81 m/s² is the standard gravitational acceleration on Earth at sea level. Using 10 is a common approximation but less precise.

faq.q: What units should I use for this calculator?
faq.a: Mass in kilograms (kg), height in meters (m), and gravity in m/s². Results display in joules (J), the SI unit for energy.
